iran_crude: Iran's crude exports averaged ~2.0 mb/d in 23–29 Mar, rebounding from a 1.6 mb/d dip in 16–22 Mar, as Kharg Island loadings continue under IRGC naval escort — Iran remains the only Gulf state loading crude through the Strait itself.

Tehran's Mar 31 Hormuz toll legislation ($2M/vessel in yuan) formalizes the chokepoint as a revenue instrument; with Trump's Apr 6 'obliteration' deadline looming, any strike on Kharg would remove the last ~2 mb/d of Iranian seaborne crude and push Brent well beyond $130.
Destination data is unavailable due to extensive AIS dark-fleet activity, but satellite imagery and Chinese port data indicate China remains the near-exclusive buyer via sanctioned tanker networks.
iran_naphtha: Iran naphtha exports collapsed to zero after 9–15 Mar (~46 kb/d to the UAE), as the Hormuz closure severed intra-Gulf product trade — the UAE was the sole buyer and has since pivoted to non-Gulf sources.
No recovery expected while Hormuz remains contested; Iran's own refining sector is prioritizing domestic fuel supply after Israeli strikes took ~12% of South Pars gas output offline.
iraq_crude: Iraq's crude exports stabilized at ~250 kb/d in 23–29 Mar — entirely via the Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline to Turkey — representing a 94% collapse from pre-war levels of 4.3 mb/d following the FM declaration on all foreign-operated oilfields.

Basra output is down to ~800–900 kb/d as storage fills; BP was ordered to cut Rumaila to 350 kb/d and the Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline (targeting 300 kb/d) is now Iraq's only functioning export route, making Turkey the sole crude buyer.
With no pipeline bypass for southern crude and tanker nominations impossible at Basra terminals, Iraq faces forced well shut-ins that could take months to reverse even if Hormuz reopens.
iraq_naphtha: Iraq naphtha exports are negligible (~13 kb/d in 23–29 Mar with no identified buyer), consistent with the near-total shutdown of Basra refining and NGL processing under the FM declaration.
No recovery path exists while southern export terminals remain inaccessible and crude production is curtailed 76% from pre-war levels.
kuwait_crude: Kuwait crude exports have effectively ceased — zero in 23–29 Mar after a brief 78 kb/d trickle in 16–22 Mar — following the KPC force majeure and Al-Ahmadi refinery drone strikes (Mar 19–20) that took 730 kb/d of refining capacity offline.

The Mar 31 drone strike on VLCC Al Salmi (2M bbl crude) at Dubai port anchorage underscores the impossibility of seaborne loadings; Kuwait has no pipeline bypass and faces 3–4 months of post-war repairs per KPC estimates.
With storage filling and no export outlet, Kuwait's entire 2.6 mb/d pre-war export capacity is stranded — further production shut-ins are imminent.
kuwait_gasoil_diesel: Kuwait diesel exports dropped to zero after 9–15 Mar (~102 kb/d to Qatar and UAE), as the Al-Ahmadi refinery destruction eliminated the primary source of Kuwaiti refined products.
Intra-Gulf product trade has ceased entirely; both Qatar and UAE — the only prior buyers — are themselves under severe supply stress from the conflict.
kuwait_gasoline: Kuwait gasoline exports zeroed out after a single cargo to Saudi Arabia (~64 kb/d in 9–15 Mar), consistent with Al-Ahmadi refinery going offline following drone strikes Mar 19–20.
No recovery possible until refinery repairs are complete — KPC estimates 3–4 months post-conflict to restore full capacity.
oman_crude: Oman has emerged as the Gulf's crisis-winner: crude exports doubled from 550 kb/d (9–15 Mar) to 1.1 mb/d (23–29 Mar) as its non-Hormuz export position at Mina Al-Fahal commands significant Asian premiums.

China is the dominant buyer at near-100% share, absorbing 1.1 mb/d in 23–29 Mar — doubling in two weeks — as Chinese refiners scramble for non-sanctioned, non-Hormuz barrels to replace lost Gulf supply.

Salalah Port resumed operations Mar 31 after the Mar 29 drone strike destroyed a crane, but inland production (~950 kb/d) remains intact — Oman's output ceiling, not logistics, is now the binding constraint on further export growth.
oman_lng: Oman LNG (~10.4 Mtpa capacity) is running steadily at 30–43 ktons/d, the only major Gulf LNG facility still operating while Qatar's 77 Mtpa sits offline — commanding significant Asian spot premiums per Platts LNG Daily.
India has emerged as the top buyer at 21.5 ktons/d in 23–29 Mar, likely replacing lost Qatar volumes under Petronet LNG's force majeure, with Thailand rotating in as a consistent secondary buyer.

With Qatar's damaged trains requiring 3–5 years for repair and remaining capacity hostage to the conflict, Oman LNG's strategic value has increased dramatically — but its ~10 Mtpa is a fraction of the ~77 Mtpa Qatar gap.
oman_kero_jet: Oman jet fuel exports rose to ~46 kb/d in 23–29 Mar (from zero in 9–15 Mar), with Djibouti as the sole buyer — likely supplying US/coalition military operations and humanitarian logistics in the Horn of Africa corridor.
This new trade lane reflects Oman's non-Hormuz advantage: Djibouti-bound cargoes avoid both the Strait of Hormuz and Bab el-Mandeb chokepoints from Oman's Gulf of Oman coast.
oman_gasoil_diesel: Oman diesel exports tripled from ~50 kb/d (9–15 Mar, Pakistan only) to ~147 kb/d (23–29 Mar), with buyers expanding to Mauritius, Mozambique, and South Africa — traditional UAE/Saudi diesel supply chains to East Africa have broken down, redirecting demand to Oman.
Pakistan remains a consistent ~50 kb/d buyer each week, while the rapid appearance of East African nations signals Oman's emergence as the Gulf's last-resort refined products supplier.
oman_gasoline: Oman gasoline exports nearly tripled from ~43 kb/d (9–15 Mar) to ~120 kb/d (23–29 Mar), with Saudi Arabia appearing as a buyer at 47 kb/d — a striking role reversal that underscores the kingdom's severe domestic refining stress with Yanbu pipeline capacity fully allocated to crude.

The rapidly expanding buyer base (Tanzania → Sudan/South Africa → Pakistan/Saudi Arabia) reflects Oman's position as the last accessible Gulf refining centre, though capacity limits will cap further growth.
oman_naphtha: Oman naphtha exports fell to zero after 9–15 Mar (~72 kb/d to South Korea) despite intact production, likely reflecting Oman's prioritization of higher-margin crude exports and domestic refining over naphtha.

South Korea's YNCC, which declared FM on ethylene due to total Middle East naphtha supply suspension, has not found replacement Omani cargoes — naphtha-dependent crackers across Asia remain severely feedstock-constrained.
qatar_crude: Qatar crude exports are effectively zero — a single ~117 kb/d cargo to the UAE in 16–22 Mar was the only loading since the conflict began, consistent with the QatarEnergy FM covering all hydrocarbon exports and all offshore fields (~440 kb/d) shut since early March.

The lone UAE-bound cargo likely represents a short-haul emergency supply arrangement from pre-loaded storage; Laffan Refineries 1 and 2 remain offline with no restart timeline until the conflict ends.
qatar_lng: Qatar LNG shipments are a trickle — rising from zero to ~18 ktons/d in 23–29 Mar, entirely to Kuwait and Pakistan — versus pre-war capacity of ~210 ktons/d (77 Mtpa), a 91% collapse in the world's largest LNG exporter.

The small volumes reaching Kuwait (via short-haul Gulf routes) and Pakistan suggest limited spot activity from pre-loaded cargoes, not resumed production — Edison's Mar 27 notification of FM on 10 LNG cargoes through mid-June confirms European buyers face months without Qatar supply.

With 12.8 Mtpa of capacity damaged for 3–5 years and remaining trains shut for security, Cheniere's US terminals (now at 110% nameplate utilization) and Oman LNG are absorbing what they can — but a ~20% global LNG supply gap persists, driving Asian spot prices past $25/MMBtu.
saudi_arabia_crude: Saudi crude exports rose steadily to ~4.4 mb/d in 23–29 Mar, up 55% from 2.8 mb/d in 9–15 Mar, as the East-West Pipeline reached full 7 mb/d throughput — 5 mb/d for export via Yanbu, 2 mb/d for domestic refining (per Aramco CEO).

Customer mix shifted dramatically: China (1.1 mb/d) and India (1.0 mb/d) dominate 23–29 Mar, while Japan, South Korea, and Egypt disappeared — replaced by Myanmar (572 kb/d) and Malaysia (148 kb/d), signalling Yanbu's Red Sea position is attracting new Southeast Asian buyers routing via Suez or Cape.

The ~4.4 mb/d rate is approaching the Yanbu port capacity ceiling of ~5 mb/d; Aramco is slashing Asia supply allocations for the second consecutive month in April, with only Arab Light available — further recovery is capped unless Hormuz reopens.
saudi_arabia_kero_jet: Saudi jet fuel exports collapsed to zero in 23–29 Mar after averaging 46–95 kb/d in prior weeks, with the UK as the only identified buyer (16–22 Mar) — Yanbu port capacity is being prioritized for crude at $110+ Brent over lower-margin jet fuel.
The pipeline throughput constraint forces a crude-vs-products trade-off at Yanbu; jet fuel cargoes are being sacrificed to maximize crude export revenue during the crisis.
saudi_arabia_gasoil_diesel: Saudi diesel exports remained robust at ~542 kb/d in 23–29 Mar, with Egypt consistently the top buyer (~115 kb/d) — Cairo's food security depends on Saudi diesel for agriculture and transport, making this a strategically prioritized flow.

The buyer mix widened to include Belgium (106 kb/d), Kenya, Pakistan, and Sudan, reflecting Saudi Arabia's emergence as the primary non-Russian refined diesel supplier for European, African, and South Asian markets during the crisis — Yanbu's Red Sea position enables both Suez-bound and Indian Ocean routes.
saudi_arabia_gasoline: Saudi gasoline exports rose 34% to ~247 kb/d in 23–29 Mar, with Kenya, Singapore, and South Africa as buyers — the complete rotation away from Gulf neighbours (Oman bought in 9–15 Mar) reflects the Hormuz blockade severing intra-Gulf refined product trade.

Singapore's consistent presence (~43 kb/d) suggests Saudi gasoline is now feeding Southeast Asian blending pools, replacing lost volumes from Bahrain's destroyed Sitra refinery (405 kb/d offline) and curtailed UAE refined output.
saudi_arabia_naphtha: Saudi naphtha exports oscillated between 73–132 kb/d with Indonesia and Japan alternating as the sole buyer each week — the single-buyer pattern reflects limited tanker availability and Yanbu port slot allocation constraints under pipeline-capacity rationing.

Japan's unusual appearance as a naphtha buyer (16–22 Mar) signals the collapse of Middle East naphtha supply from UAE/Qatar/Kuwait is forcing Japanese petrochemical crackers to secure Yanbu-origin cargoes at significant premiums.
uae_crude: UAE crude exports surged to 3.2 mb/d in 23–29 Mar from 1.2 mb/d the prior week, as ADCOP pipeline flows through Fujairah averaged a record 1.62 mb/d in March — India (714 kb/d), South Korea (543 kb/d), and Japan (500 kb/d) returned as buyers after disappearing in 16–22 Mar.

The volatile week-to-week swings (2.2 → 1.2 → 3.2 mb/d) reflect sporadic Fujairah loading disruptions from drone attacks on storage tanks and pumping infrastructure, with each successful window triggering a burst of catch-up exports.

S&P Platts launched Inside/Outside Strait pricing bifurcation on Apr 1 with 9 new daily assessments, formally splitting the market between Fujairah-loadable Murban and stranded Persian Gulf grades — ADNOC deferred May–Sep maintenance to maximize Murban output via ADCOP.
uae_gasoil_diesel: UAE diesel exports collapsed to zero after 9–15 Mar (~73 kb/d to Pakistan), with Ruwais Refinery-2 offline and the Shah Gas Field shutdown (~1 Bcf/d) eliminating NGL-linked refined product flows.

Fujairah's ADCOP pipeline capacity is being fully allocated to crude rather than refined products — the Habshan gas complex resumed Mar 23 but downstream product exports remain constrained by the Hormuz blockade and prioritization of crude revenue.
uae_naphtha: UAE naphtha exports were zero for most of March, with a single ~46 kb/d cargo to Taiwan in 23–29 Mar — Ruwais Refinery-2 offline and Shah Gas shutdown have eliminated the UAE's primary naphtha production sources.
Taiwan's purchase is likely an opportunistic spot cargo from Fujairah storage rather than resumed production; structural UAE naphtha supply cannot recover until Ruwais restarts post-conflict.
eu_27_crude: EU-27 crude re-exports are marginal at ~0.1–0.2 mb/d and highly erratic — daily averages swung from 198 kbd (9–15 Mar) to just 46 kbd (16–22 Mar) before recovering to 144 kbd (23–29 Mar), reflecting opportunistic cargo timing rather than structural trade.

Zero Gulf-origin crude in any week; volumes are intra-European redistribution (Finland, UK, Netherlands, Turkey) of North Sea and pipeline-delivered barrels, with no role in offsetting Hormuz disruption.
eu_27_kero_jet: EU-27 jet fuel exports collapsed 77% from 146 kbd (9–15 Mar) to just 34 kbd (23–29 Mar), consistent with European refiners retaining product domestically as Middle East refined product supply chains fractured and aviation fuel margins surged.

Destination mix narrowed sharply — from four buyers (France, Spain, UK, Belgium) in early March to just three (Norway, UK, Malta) in the latest week, suggesting European airlines and militaries are absorbing regional supply.
eu_27_gasoil_diesel: EU-27 diesel exports halved from ~1.1 mb/d (9–22 Mar) to 527 kbd (23–29 Mar) as European refiners pulled barrels back into a tightening domestic market, with Russian diesel still under EU embargo and ME product flows near zero through Hormuz.
Poland — the top W11 buyer at 245 kbd/d — dropped to 49 kbd/d by 23–29 Mar, while Croatia surged to 96 kbd/d, pointing to shifting pipeline-vs-seaborne logistics across Central and Southern Europe.

Non-European buyers (Morocco, Lebanon, Togo, Mauritania) that received ~100 kbd/d in early March were squeezed out by 23–29 Mar, signalling European refiners are prioritising intra-bloc supply security over export revenue.
eu_27_gasoline: EU-27 gasoline exports held broadly stable at ~1.2 mb/d across March, making Europe the crisis-era swing supplier for fuel-starved markets from West Africa to the Eastern Mediterranean — Kenya (111 kbd/d), Ghana (99 kbd/d), Egypt (111 kbd/d), and Libya (40–83 kbd/d) all drew heavily.

Saudi Arabia appeared as a gasoline import destination at 38 kbd/d in 9–15 Mar — an extraordinary reversal for the world's largest refiner, consistent with Aramco's struggle to maintain domestic fuel supply with Ras Tanura and Jubail curtailed by Hormuz closure.
US demand for EU gasoline emerged at 49–91 kbd/d, alongside Puerto Rico and US Virgin Islands, as US Gulf Coast refiners face margin pressure from $112+ Brent and domestic gasoline hits $4/gal.
eu_27_naphtha: EU-27 naphtha exports remained stable at 335–413 kbd/d, with China and Japan emerging as direct buyers (73 kbd/d and 16–32 kbd/d respectively) — reflecting Asian petrochemical crackers scrambling for non-ME feedstock after Qatar condensate and Saudi naphtha effectively vanished.

Italy (60–72 kbd/d) and Spain (25–160 kbd/d) are major intra-European recipients, feeding Mediterranean petchem complexes that lost access to ME naphtha; South Korea's YNCC FM on ethylene production underscores the global feedstock crunch driving these flows.
russia_crude: Russian crude exports declined 21% from 6.7 mb/d (9–15 Mar) to 5.3 mb/d (23–29 Mar), even as the US 30-day sanctions waiver (crude loaded before Mar 12) was expected to boost flows — suggesting logistical constraints and port/tanker bottlenecks rather than demand softness.

China remained the dominant buyer but fell sharply from 2.4 mb/d to 1.3 mb/d over the three weeks, while Turkey surged from 149 kbd/d to 666 kbd/d — emerging as Russia's third-largest crude customer and a likely re-export hub for sanctions-constrained barrels.

New crisis-era buyers appeared: the Philippines took 212 kbd/d in 16–22 Mar (first-ever significant Russian crude imports), and Israel received 105 kbd/d in early March, both consistent with desperate non-Gulf sourcing after Hormuz closure removed ~17 mb/d from market.

European flows persist: Italy (657–686 kbd/d), France (303–455 kbd/d), and Netherlands (153–435 kbd/d) continue buying Russian crude despite sanctions — the war in Iran has effectively sidelined enforcement as energy security takes precedence.
russia_lng: Russian LNG exports averaged 81–111 kt/d in March, with the EU taking the bulk (France, Belgium, Spain, Netherlands) despite the April 25 EU ban on Russian LNG purchases — buyers are likely frontloading cargoes before the cutoff, creating a cliff-edge supply risk for European gas markets in May.

China's intake was volatile (9→28→0→64 kt/d), reflecting arbitrage-driven cargo diversion between Asian spot premiums and EU contract commitments; with Qatar's 77 Mtpa offline, Russian Arctic LNG volumes carry outsized pricing leverage.

Yamal LNG running at capacity while Arctic LNG-2 remains below nameplate — Russia is capturing windfall margins from the Qatar gap but structurally cannot scale further to fill the ~20% global LNG supply deficit.
russia_gasoil_diesel: Russian diesel exports collapsed 73% from ~1.0 mb/d (9–15 Mar) to just 272 kbd (23–29 Mar), with Turkey absorbing the majority of remaining flows (123 kbd/d) while Brazil, Egypt, and Syria all dropped out as buyers.

The sharp decline contrasts with surging global diesel demand — likely reflecting Russian refinery maintenance cycles and rail/port logistics constraints at Black Sea terminals, not a lack of willing buyers at current crisis-era price levels.
russia_gasoline: Russian gasoline exports are negligible — a single 38 kbd/d cargo to Libya in 9–15 Mar was the only flow in the entire month, consistent with Russia's structural domestic gasoline deficit and seasonal export ban to protect internal supply.
russia_naphtha: Russian naphtha exports declined 47% from 665 kbd/d (9–15 Mar) to 351 kbd/d (23–29 Mar), with China disappearing entirely as a buyer in the latest week despite being the dominant customer at 231 kbd/d earlier — possibly reflecting China's refined fuel export ban redirecting domestic naphtha to local crackers.

Indonesia emerged as a significant new buyer at 115 kbd/d (16–22 Mar), consistent with Chandra Asri's FM-driven pivot to non-ME naphtha sources; India remained steady at 37–82 kbd/d, diversifying feedstock away from shuttered Gulf condensate.
us_crude: US crude exports surged 13% to 4.3 mb/d (23–29 Mar) — the highest weekly rate in March — as global buyers scrambled for non-OPEC, non-Hormuz barrels with Brent at $112 and the Strait effectively closed to ~95% of prior traffic.

Asia pivoted hard to US crude: Japan emerged at 286–298 kbd/d (up from zero pre-crisis), China appeared at 224 kbd/d in 23–29 Mar, and India climbed steadily to 249 kbd/d — all consistent with SPR-supplemented but insufficient domestic reserves forcing trans-Pacific sourcing at premium freight rates.

The customer base diversified dramatically — 14 countries in 23–29 Mar including first-time buyers like Denmark (206 kbd/d), Peru (71 kbd/d), and Colombia (71 kbd/d) — as the Gulf crisis reshapes global crude trade routes that were stable for decades.

South Korea remained the top Asian buyer at 506–807 kbd/d, drawing heavily on US light sweet grades to offset the loss of ~93.5% ME-dependent crude slate and support its 22.5 mb SPR release programme.
us_lng: US LNG exports flatlined at ~370 kt/d across all three weeks — effectively at nameplate capacity with all 8 terminals (including Sabine Pass at 110% utilisation per Cheniere) running flat out, meaning the US cannot meaningfully increase supply to fill Qatar's ~77 Mtpa gap.

Destination mix shifted toward Asia: Thailand (34 kt/d), Bangladesh (10 kt/d), and India (20 kt/d) all appeared or expanded as buyers in the latest weeks, consistent with Asian spot premiums outpacing European bids by $1–3/MMBtu as Qatar's FM extended to Edison and all major long-term buyers.

Europe still claims the largest share (Netherlands 42–67 kt/d, France 31–73 kt/d, Germany 10–31 kt/d), but the April 25 EU ban on Russian LNG will force European buyers to bid even more aggressively for US cargoes — a looming competition that could spike global LNG prices further.
us_lpg: US LPG exports rebounded to 169 kt/d (23–29 Mar) from a dip of 128 kt/d the prior week, as Enterprise and Targa Gulf Coast terminals ramped shipments to fill the void left by Saudi, UAE, and Qatari NGL shutdowns — the world's three largest ME LPG exporters are all offline.

Japan remained the top and most consistent buyer at 28–33 kt/d, but China (14 kt/d) and Indonesia (13 kt/d) surged in 23–29 Mar — both are historically ME-dependent LPG importers now forced into costly trans-Pacific arbitrage as PDH plant economics deteriorate.
Egypt appeared as a new US LPG buyer (6–25 kt/d) reflecting the cascading impact of Gulf disruption on Mediterranean supply chains traditionally fed by Saudi and UAE propane.
us_kero_jet: US jet fuel exports rose from 214 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 285–322 kbd (16–29 Mar) as Latin American and European buyers compensated for lost ME jet supply — the UK became a consistent buyer at 44–84 kbd/d, likely supporting military operations and transatlantic aviation demand.

The customer base is overwhelmingly Western Hemisphere (Mexico, Brazil, Peru, Honduras, Costa Rica, Panama, Ecuador), suggesting US jet fuel is not yet reaching the most acutely affected Asian markets where ME kerosene shortages are driving refining margin spikes.
us_gasoil_diesel: US diesel exports surged 31% to 1.58 mb/d (23–29 Mar) — the highest weekly rate in March — as the Netherlands became the dominant buyer at 397 kbd/d (up from 9 kbd/d in W11), establishing Rotterdam as the key re-distribution hub for US diesel flowing into diesel-starved Europe.

Australia appeared as a consistent diesel buyer at 43–87 kbd/d across all three weeks, an unusual trans-Pacific flow reflecting Australia's leveraging of its LNG export leverage to secure fuel supplies (per Bloomberg) as the Iran war disrupts traditional Asian diesel supply from ME refineries.

Latin America drew steadily: Peru (102–162 kbd/d), Chile (65–157 kbd/d), and Mexico (34–133 kbd/d) all relied on US Gulf Coast diesel, underscoring that the Hormuz crisis has made the US the default swing supplier for the entire Western Hemisphere.
us_gasoline: US gasoline exports declined ~12% from 815 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 717 kbd (23–29 Mar), consistent with domestic supply tightening as US pump prices hit $4/gal — the highest since 2022 — creating political pressure to retain product at home rather than export.

Mexico's share fell sharply from 311 kbd/d to 152 kbd/d, likely reflecting reduced purchasing power as the peso weakened and Mexico's own Pemex refineries increased throughput to reduce import dependence.

Australia appeared as a first-time US gasoline buyer at 64 kbd/d (16–22 Mar), another sign of how deeply the Hormuz closure has disrupted traditional Asia-Pacific fuel supply chains that relied on Singapore and ME product hubs.
us_naphtha: US naphtha exports spiked 76% to 738 kbd (16–22 Mar) before falling back to 386 kbd (23–29 Mar), driven by a massive South Korean purchase of 203 kbd/d — consistent with YNCC's FM-driven naphtha cracker emergency and Seoul's ban on naphtha re-exports (enforced Mar 27) to retain feedstock.

Venezuela re-emerged as a major buyer at 107 kbd/d (23–29 Mar) after a week's absence, reflecting the easing of some bilateral trade restrictions as the global naphtha shortage forces pragmatic supply relationships.

Japan's purchases declined from 114 kbd/d to 32 kbd/d over the month, potentially reflecting the draw on strategic reserves rather than increased spot purchases, even as Japanese crackers face the same ME feedstock loss.
australia_crude: Australian crude exports declined 35% from 280 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 183 kbd (23–29 Mar), with South Korea the dominant buyer at 106 kbd/d — Australia's small production base (~350 kbd total output) means it cannot materially offset the Gulf supply loss despite commanding premium pricing.

Japan appeared as buyer in 16–29 Mar (30–81 kbd/d), reflecting Tokyo's diversification away from its 93.5% ME crude dependency and the parallel 80 mb SPR release — every non-Gulf barrel counts when you've released the largest strategic stockpile in national history.
australia_lng: Australian LNG exports dropped 33% from 252 kt/d (9–15 Mar) to 169 kt/d (23–29 Mar) after Cyclone Narelle knocked NW Shelf, Gorgon (1 train), and Wheatstone offline — catastrophic timing given Australia's role as the world's de facto largest active LNG exporter with Qatar's 77 Mtpa shut.

Wheatstone is expected to take weeks to return to full rates as damage assessments continue, while Gorgon resumed full production Mar 29; combined, these three plants account for ~8.4% of global LNG trade — their partial outage compounds an already historic ~20% global supply gap.

India appeared as a new Australian LNG buyer (8 kt/d in 23–29 Mar) for the first time in the dataset, consistent with Petronet LNG's FM-driven scramble for alternative supply after Qatar — which supplied 41% of India's LNG imports — went entirely offline.

Japan remained the anchor buyer at 48–71 kt/d, with its Daiwa Institute warning that a 10% ME LNG import cut would push GDP negative — securing Australian contract volumes is now an existential economic priority, not merely an energy procurement decision.
australia_naphtha: Australian naphtha exports are sporadic and minimal (~49 kbd in two of four weeks, zero in the others), confined to a single Singapore buyer — Australia lacks meaningful naphtha export infrastructure and cannot contribute to filling the acute Asian petchem feedstock gap.
china_crude: China's crude imports contracted 29% from ~11.4 mb/d (9–15 Mar) to ~8.0 mb/d (23–29 Mar), as Gulf supply collapsed: Saudi Arabia and UAE disappeared entirely in the latest week, and Iraq plunged from 781 to 73 kbd — consistent with Basra's FM-driven output crash to 900k bpd and tanker nomination impossibility.

Russia remains the anchor supplier at ~2.2 mb/d throughout, with pipeline flows (ESPO ~600 kbd, Kazakhstan ~220 kbd, Myanmar ~200 kbd) providing ~1.0 mb/d of Hormuz-independent baseline. Brazil surged to second-largest supplier at 1.1 mb/d (23–29 Mar), up from near-zero pre-crisis.

Gulf share eroded from 43.7% to 31.1% over three weeks. Oman — the only non-Hormuz Gulf exporter — surged to 1.5 mb/d (23–29 Mar) from 0.6 mb/d, now China's second-largest Gulf source. Per Vortexa, China's ~1 billion barrels of onshore inventory provides months of buffer, but drawdown is accelerating.

Iran continues flowing at ~0.9 mb/d (23–29 Mar), likely via IRGC-tolled passage; however, Iran's new $2M/vessel yuan toll and Trump's Apr 6 deadline to reopen Hormuz or face military action on Kharg could abruptly end even these volumes.
china_lng: China LNG imports collapsed 39% from 134 kt/d (9–15 Mar) to 82 kt/d (23–29 Mar) as QatarEnergy's FM — extended to Chinese contracts on Mar 24 — zeroed out Qatar supply entirely by late March. Qatar had provided 45 kt/d (34% share) just two weeks earlier.

Australia has become the dominant supplier at 37 kt/d (46% of W13 imports), with Malaysia (15 kt/d), PNG (11 kt/d), and Russia (9 kt/d) filling partial gaps. The ~52 kt/d Qatar gap far exceeds available spot volumes; Asian spot LNG prices surged ~39% past $25/MMBtu per Platts.

With all 77 Mtpa of Qatari LNG halted since Mar 2 and 12.8 Mtpa physically damaged for 3–5 years, China's Power of Siberia pipeline gas provides partial insulation but cannot replace seaborne volumes. Wanhua Chemical's FM on 2.2 Mtpa ethylene capacity at Yantai underscores downstream cascading impact.
china_lpg: China LPG imports show high volatility (61→47→64 kt/d across three weeks) as the supplier mix reshuffles: US surged to 32 kt/d (50% share) in 23–29 Mar, while Iran persists at ~15 kt/d via tolled Hormuz passage. Saudi, Kuwait, and Qatar LPG — byproducts of now-offline NGL processing — have effectively vanished.

As the world's largest LPG importer, China's PDH plant operators are scrambling for US and Canadian cargoes at sharply higher prices. Canada doubled to 13 kt/d in 23–29 Mar. The LPG-naphtha spread has shifted as both feedstocks face Gulf-linked constraints, per ICIS.
china_kero_jet: China's jet/kerosene imports effectively ceased after a single 24 kbd cargo from South Korea in 9–15 Mar. China banned refined fuel exports on Mar 4–5 and Sinopec/Zhejiang Petrochemical are trimming throughput 10%+, suggesting domestic supply is prioritized for internal consumption over trade.
china_gasoil_diesel: China's gasoil/diesel imports remain minimal at 10–15 kbd, sourced entirely from South Korea (with one week of Japanese supply). Volumes are insignificant relative to China's ~14 mb/d refining capacity; the fuel export ban since Mar 4 confirms China is hoarding refined products domestically rather than importing.
china_naphtha: China's naphtha imports nearly halved from 409 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 215 kbd (23–29 Mar), with Gulf supply collapsing from 46% to zero. Kuwait (124 kbd), Qatar, Bahrain, and UAE all disappeared as Middle East NGL processing and refinery operations shut down under successive FM declarations.

By 23–29 Mar, China sourced naphtha exclusively from non-Gulf origins: Norway (77 kbd), South Korea (49 kbd), Thailand (48 kbd), and Russia (42 kbd) — a complete geographic restructuring in under three weeks. With naphtha surging past $1,000/mt per market reports, downstream ethylene and propylene costs are spiking, threatening ~50% of global PE capacity per Atlantic Council estimates.
india_crude: India's crude imports recovered from a crisis low of ~3.6 mb/d (9–15 Mar) to ~4.3 mb/d (23–29 Mar), with Operation Urja Suraksha's 7-warship naval escort convoy enabling Gulf barrels to resume flowing. Gulf share surged from a nadir of 5.8% to 35.1%, with Saudi Arabia (951 kbd) and UAE (486 kbd) reappearing strongly.

Russia remains dominant at 1.7 mb/d (39% share in 23–29 Mar), nearly double pre-crisis levels, with India-Russia crude supply talks restarted Mar 19. Iraq has essentially disappeared (61 kbd vs. pre-war capacity of 4.3 mb/d exports), consistent with Basra's FM and storage reaching capacity limits.

India's crude trade deficit is projected to widen by >$4B in March amid Brent's record 55% monthly surge. Iran's 'friendly nation' safe-passage status for India and naval convoy success are partially offsetting Hormuz risk, but volumes remain ~15–20% below India's ~5 mb/d pre-crisis import rate.
india_lng: India LNG imports swung violently — from 35 kt/d (9–15 Mar) to 31 kt/d (16–22 Mar) then surging to 79 kt/d (23–29 Mar). Qatar is completely absent across all weeks despite supplying 41.4% of India's LNG pre-crisis, reflecting Petronet LNG's FM declaration after QatarEnergy's global halt.

Oman's non-Hormuz LNG terminal emerged as India's lifeline at 32 kt/d (41% share in 23–29 Mar), supplemented by US (19 kt/d) and Nigerian (18 kt/d) spot cargoes. Gujarat Gas's 50% industrial supply cut remains in force; India's Petroleum Ministry assured 'sufficient stock' on Mar 27, but the structural Qatar gap persists.
india_lpg: India's navy-escorted LPG convoys (Pine Gas and Jag Vasant, 92,000t combined) reached Indian ports Mar 26–28 under Operation Urja Suraksha, keeping Gulf LPG flowing at reduced levels. Gulf share nonetheless fell from 48% to 35% (23–29 Mar) as US supply doubled to 10.5 kt/d.

Total LPG imports stabilized at ~16 kt/d (23–29 Mar), matching early-March levels after a mid-month dip to 12 kt/d. The mix is shifting structurally toward US Gulf Coast cargoes from Enterprise/Targa terminals, which benefit from the LPG-naphtha spread favouring increased US exports per Argus.
india_naphtha: Russia has become India's dominant naphtha supplier, surging from 53 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 129 kbd (23–29 Mar) — a 142% increase. In 16–22 Mar, Russia was the sole supplier (100% share) after UAE naphtha flows ceased following Fujairah and Ruwais disruptions.

Total naphtha imports surged 73% to 175 kbd (23–29 Mar) as Oman (46 kbd, non-Hormuz) supplemented Russian volumes. MRPL's FM on gasoline exports and 500 kbd refinery throughput cuts show Indian refiners are reshuffling crude slate to maximize domestic naphtha yield where possible.
japan_crude: Japan faces the most severe supply crisis among Asian importers: crude imports collapsed 49% from ~2.1 mb/d (9–15 Mar) to ~1.1 mb/d (23–29 Mar), with Gulf dependence locked at 86–100% — the highest of any major importer. Japan has zero Russian crude and minimal non-Gulf diversification.

The 80-million-barrel SPR release (Japan's largest ever, started Mar 16) is now the primary supply cushion, with oil flowing from 10 national stockpile sites. Saudi Arabia (611 kbd) and UAE (395 kbd) remain the only meaningful suppliers in 23–29 Mar, but at half their early-March rates. METI warned additional releases may be needed if disruptions persist past April.

Iraq, Kuwait, and Oman all disappeared from Japan's supplier list by 23–29 Mar. With ME crude dependence at 93.5% pre-crisis and no pipeline alternatives, Japan is considering Russian crude purchases per the Moscow Times — a dramatic policy shift if realized.
japan_lng: Japan's LNG imports declined 17% from 201 kt/d (9–15 Mar) to 167 kt/d (23–29 Mar), with Australia consolidating dominance at 111 kt/d (67% share in W13). Qatar dropped from 10.7 kt/d to zero. Daiwa Institute warns that a 10% ME LNG cut alone could push Japan's GDP negative.

Notably, China re-exported LNG to Japan at 21 kt/d in 16–22 Mar — an unusual arbitrage reflecting the scramble for flexible cargoes. Gulf share fell to just 5.8% (23–29 Mar) as Japan pivoted to Australian, Malaysian, and PNG contracts. US spot cargoes are being diverted eastward, with Asian spot now outpacing European gains by $1–3/MMBtu per Natural Gas Intelligence.
japan_lpg: Japan's LPG imports are now 100% US-sourced across all three weeks (27→18→35 kt/d), a complete pivot from pre-crisis Gulf dependence. The volume surge to 35 kt/d in 23–29 Mar reflects new US Gulf Coast supply contracts filling the Saudi/Qatar NGL gap, but at significant cost premiums per Argus.

Zero Gulf LPG for three consecutive weeks confirms the total shutdown of Saudi, Kuwait, and Qatar NGL-linked exports. Japan's petrochemical and residential heating sectors face structurally higher feedstock costs for the foreseeable future.
japan_kero_jet: Japan's jet fuel imports are entirely sourced from South Korea at 31–73 kbd, with the intra-Asian refining supply chain functioning normally. South Korean refiners are effectively backstopping Japan's aviation fuel needs as Japanese refineries cut throughput due to Gulf crude shortages.
japan_gasoil_diesel: Japan's gasoil/diesel imports averaged 15–53 kbd, sourced primarily from South Korea with a one-off Brunei cargo (50 kbd in 16–22 Mar). Volumes are low and entirely intra-Asian, reflecting Japan's domestic refining capacity meeting most diesel demand despite reduced crude throughput.
japan_gasoline: Japan's gasoline imports from South Korea tripled from 27 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 85 kbd (23–29 Mar), signalling that Japanese refineries are cutting gasoline output as Gulf crude supply dwindles. South Korea's refining complex is becoming Japan's de facto product supplier across gasoline, jet fuel, and diesel.
japan_naphtha: Japan's naphtha imports collapsed 70% to 86 kbd (16–22 Mar) when Qatar was the sole supplier, then partially recovered to 225 kbd (23–29 Mar) as UAE stepped in at 170 kbd. Kuwait (70 kbd in W11) disappeared entirely, consistent with Al-Ahmadi refinery drone strikes and KPC's FM.

Gulf naphtha share fluctuated wildly (41%→100%→76%) reflecting Japan's inability to source non-Gulf alternatives at scale. With naphtha surging past $1,000/mt globally, Japan's petrochemical crackers face feedstock costs 60%+ above pre-crisis levels. About 42% of Japan's naphtha historically comes from the Middle East, per C&EN.
south_korea_crude: South Korea's crude imports plunged 55% from ~3.2 mb/d (9–15 Mar) to ~1.4 mb/d (23–29 Mar). The SPR reserve swap launched Mar 31, providing 20+ mb of crude to refiners in Apr–May, acknowledges that market supply alone cannot bridge the gap. A record 22.5 mb SPR release was announced Mar 11.

US crude surged to 807 kbd (37% share) in 16–22 Mar before pulling back to 150 kbd as available cargoes tightened. Gulf share bounced to 67% (23–29 Mar) as UAE (589 kbd) became the top supplier — likely ADCOP pipeline-exported Murban barrels bypassing Hormuz at record 1.62 mb/d loadings.

Iraq fell from 550 to 136 kbd over three weeks; Kuwait disappeared entirely after W12. South Korea's naphtha export ban (Mar 27) signals downstream rationing as crude throughput falls. Algeria (154 kbd) and Canada (83 kbd) appeared as new, small-volume diversification plays in 23–29 Mar.
south_korea_lng: South Korea's LNG imports declined 44% from 200 kt/d (9–15 Mar) to 112 kt/d (23–29 Mar). Qatar dropped from 17 kt/d to zero by W13, following QatarEnergy's FM extension to South Korean contracts on Mar 24. KOGAS reports inventory exceeds mandatory reserves, providing a short-term buffer.

Australia (48 kt/d), Malaysia (21 kt/d), and US (18 kt/d) anchor the diversified supply base. Notably, China is re-exporting LNG to South Korea at 8–22 kt/d — likely reselling surplus pipeline-backed gas as spot cargoes. Woodside's 0.5 Mtpa deal (10.5-year term, commenced 2026) and TotalEnergies' 1 Mtpa deal provide medium-term insulation.
south_korea_lpg: South Korea's LPG imports are 100% North American (US + Canada) across all three weeks, with zero Gulf supply. However, total volumes halved from 17.8 to 8.2 kt/d (23–29 Mar), suggesting tightening US export availability as multiple Asian buyers compete for the same Enterprise/Targa cargoes.

With Saudi, UAE, and Qatari NGL processing shut down under FM declarations, the LPG-naphtha price spread has shifted as both feedstocks face Gulf-linked constraints. South Korea's PDH and naphtha cracker operators face compounding feedstock shortages.
south_korea_gasoil_diesel: South Korea's diesel imports are minimal (7–65 kbd) and entirely intra-Asian, sourced from Japan, China, Russia, and Taiwan. The W12 spike to 65 kbd (Japan 50 kbd) likely reflects one-off cargo timing rather than structural demand. South Korea's own refining complex handles most domestic diesel needs.
south_korea_gasoline: South Korea's gasoline imports collapsed from 108 kbd (9–15 Mar, all from Japan) to zero by 23–29 Mar. South Korea is a net refined-products exporter and its refineries are prioritizing domestic supply as crude inputs fall. The naphtha export ban (Mar 27) confirms a shift toward internal rationing.
south_korea_naphtha: South Korea's naphtha imports fell 30% from 490 to 344 kbd (23–29 Mar), with a dramatic geographic restructuring: Gulf share collapsed from 95.6% to 30.1% in three weeks. UAE and Qatar — which supplied 401 kbd (82% combined) in W11 — were largely replaced by Mediterranean (Greece 62 kbd, Egypt 47 kbd), North Africa (Algeria 53 kbd, Libya 27 kbd), and South American (Peru 49 kbd) cargoes.

YNCC's FM on ethylene production due to 'total Middle East naphtha supply suspension' underscores the severity. Oman emerged as the last reliable Gulf source at 70 kbd (23–29 Mar). With naphtha above $1,000/mt and the naphtha export ban in force, South Korea's petrochemical sector faces its worst feedstock crisis in decades.
thailand_crude: Thailand's crude imports fell 37% to ~785 kbd (23–29 Mar) from 1,252 kbd in 9–15 Mar, with the supplier base collapsing from 7 countries to just 2 — UAE now accounts for 93.5% of all inflows after the US, Brazil, Libya, and Australia all dropped out.

Bangkok's bilateral safe-passage deal with Tehran (signed 28 Mar) has kept UAE crude flowing through Hormuz, but this dependency on a single fragile corridor is precarious — any escalation at the Strait would instantly zero out Thailand's entire crude supply.

The disappearance of US crude (which was 449 kbd in 9–15 Mar) suggests American barrels are being redirected to higher-premium buyers; Thailand is reportedly preparing to open Russian crude negotiations as a hedge against further Gulf disruption.
thailand_lng: Thailand's LNG imports have fully bypassed the Gulf crisis — zero Qatar volumes across all four weeks, with Australia emerging as the anchor supplier at ~20 ktons/d, supplemented by rotating cargoes from Canada, Mozambique, Malaysia, and China.

Oman appeared as a new Gulf-adjacent LNG source in 23–29 Mar (10 ktons/d), leveraging its non-Hormuz export position from the 10.4 Mtpa facility that is commanding significant Asian spot premiums with Qatar's 77 Mtpa offline.

Supply diversification is working but at sharply higher cost — Asian spot LNG has surged past $24/MMBtu (nearly doubling from pre-crisis ~$10), and Thailand's growing pipeline gas dependence (declining Gulf of Thailand/Myanmar output) compounds the vulnerability.
thailand_gasoil_diesel: Thailand's diesel imports are minimal at 8–46 kbd, sourced entirely from regional refiners (Malaysia and India) with zero Gulf exposure — reflecting adequate domestic refining capacity for middle distillates.
India's one-off diesel cargo (46 kbd in 16–22 Mar) suggests MRPL or other Indian refiners are redirecting product exports to Southeast Asia despite their own FM-constrained throughput cuts.
thailand_gasoline: Thailand's gasoline imports nearly tripled from 5.4 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 15.7 kbd (23–29 Mar), sourced exclusively from Singapore and Malaysia — consistent with tightening domestic fuel supply as crude feedstock shrinks.

Zero Gulf gasoline exposure means Thailand is insulated from direct product disruption, but the rising import trend signals that falling crude throughput is beginning to constrain domestic refinery gasoline output.
thailand_naphtha: Thailand's naphtha supply collapsed after Bahrain's Bapco Sitra refinery (405 kb/d) was destroyed by missile strikes — Bahrain was the sole supplier at 36.7 kbd in 9–15 Mar, then volumes dropped to zero in 16–22 Mar before a token 3.2 kbd from Singapore in 23–29 Mar.

This is a direct FM cascading impact: Bapco Energies declared force majeure on all operations after the second Sitra strike, eliminating Thailand's entire naphtha import channel and threatening petrochemical feedstock security.
vietnam_crude: Vietnam's crude imports have effectively collapsed to zero — from 286 kbd (all from Kuwait) in 9–15 Mar, to a single 88 kbd Nigerian cargo in 16–22 Mar, to nothing in the final two weeks of March.

This is a direct consequence of KPC's force majeure and the Hormuz closure: Kuwait supplied ~80% of Vietnam's crude, primarily feeding the Nghi Son refinery (NSRP), which confirmed its last Kuwait shipment was mid-March with subsequent schedules 'affected by the Strait situation'.

Hanoi's emergency response has been aggressive — cutting MFN oil import tariffs to 0% (Decree 72/2026, effective 9 Mar), arranging 4 million barrels from international partners, and PM Chinh directly calling Kuwait's PM to reaffirm supply — but the flow data shows these measures have not yet restored physical crude arrivals.
Nghi Son claims crude stocks sufficient through end of May, but without new cargoes arriving, Vietnam faces a refinery shutdown scenario by June that would ripple across domestic fuel supply.
vietnam_kero_jet: Vietnam's jet fuel imports are small (10–27 kbd) and sourced entirely from China and Singapore, with no Gulf exposure — volumes fluctuate week-to-week but the supply chain appears stable.
Singapore appeared as a second source in 23–29 Mar (13.3 kbd alongside China's 13.7 kbd), providing some diversification for Vietnam's aviation fuel needs.
vietnam_gasoil_diesel: Vietnam's diesel imports nearly tripled from 77 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 227 kbd (23–29 Mar), signalling an urgent product-import pivot as crude feedstock for domestic refining dries up — South Korea dominates at ~167 kbd, with Taiwan appearing as a new supplier (48 kbd) in the latest week.

This surge is the downstream consequence of Vietnam's crude supply collapse: with Nghi Son and Dung Quat refineries facing feedstock constraints, Vietnam is importing finished diesel at crisis-premium prices to keep transport and industry running.

South Korea's role as Vietnam's diesel lifeline is notable given Seoul's own naphtha export ban (27 Mar) and SPR drawdowns — raising questions about how long Korea can sustain this level of product exports.
vietnam_gasoline: Vietnam's gasoline imports fell 46% from 43 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 23 kbd (23–29 Mar), sourced almost entirely from Singapore after Japan disappeared as a supplier — a concerning decline as domestic crude throughput shrinks.

The divergence between surging diesel imports and falling gasoline imports suggests Vietnam is prioritising industrial/transport fuel over motor gasoline, or that Singapore's refinery complex is allocating product to higher-bidding markets.
vietnam_naphtha: Vietnam imported zero naphtha for three consecutive weeks (9–29 Mar), then received a single Russian cargo of 338 kbd on 30 Mar — a structural shift consistent with the broader Asian pivot to Russian hydrocarbons as Gulf supply evaporates.
The Russian naphtha arrival is one of the earliest signs of Moscow filling the petrochemical feedstock gap in Southeast Asia, alongside Thailand's nascent Russian crude negotiations.
united_states_crude: US crude imports are running at ~2.6 mb/d (23–29 Mar), down 18% from 3.1 mb/d two weeks earlier, with the Western Hemisphere providing the backbone: Venezuela (~389 kbd), Mexico (~280 kbd), Brazil (~294 kbd), and Canada (~173 kbd) account for 44% of inflows.

Gulf crude continues arriving via non-Hormuz routes — Saudi Arabia delivered ~526 kbd (23–29 Mar) likely via Yanbu/East-West Pipeline, and Iraq supplied ~170 kbd via Kirkuk-Ceyhan, keeping Gulf share at 27% despite the Strait closure.

The supplier base has diversified dramatically to include Ghana, Ecuador, Argentina, and Guyana alongside traditional sources — consistent with refiners scrambling for medium-sour barrels to replace lost Gulf heavy grades, with US gasoline hitting $4/gal on 31 Mar for the first time since 2022.

With 45.2 mb of the 172 mb SPR release delivered and options traders betting on $150/bbl by end-April, the declining import trend suggests the SPR drawdown is partially substituting for waterborne arrivals rather than supplementing them.
united_states_kero_jet: US jet fuel imports are sourced entirely from the Pacific Rim — South Korea dominates at ~69 kbd (23–29 Mar), with Canada and Taiwan providing supplementary volumes, and zero Gulf or Middle East exposure.

The halving of jet imports from 204 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 101 kbd (23–29 Mar) likely reflects both reduced Asian surplus availability and US refiners maximising domestic jet output as crude import slates shift.
united_states_gasoil_diesel: US diesel imports fell 60% from 242 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 96 kbd (23–29 Mar), sourced almost entirely from Canada (~93 kbd) — Turkey disappeared as a supplier after its initial 64 kbd cargo, leaving the US heavily dependent on a single pipeline/cross-border source.

With no Gulf diesel flowing and China's refined fuel export ban removing ~$22B/yr of Asian swing supply, the tightening Atlantic Basin diesel market is a key driver behind the broader energy price surge.
united_states_gasoline: US gasoline imports held relatively stable at ~306 kbd (23–29 Mar), with India emerging as a notable new supplier at 44 kbd — an unusual flow suggesting Indian refiners (despite MRPL's FM) are redirecting surplus gasoline to the premium US market.

Canada remains the top gasoline source (109 kbd) alongside Caribbean/Bahamas transshipment volumes (74 kbd), while South Korea (25 kbd) and the UK (12 kbd) provide additional barrels — a globally diversified product slate with zero Gulf dependence.
united_states_naphtha: US naphtha sourcing shifted dramatically from European refiners (Belgium, Netherlands, France at 67 kbd in 9–15 Mar) to Atlantic Basin producers (Angola 49 kbd, Brazil 36 kbd, Argentina 11 kbd) by 23–29 Mar — total volumes actually rose 43% to 96 kbd.

The pivot away from European naphtha toward African and Latin American supply likely reflects competition from European petrochemical crackers bidding up continental naphtha as their own Gulf feedstock disappears.
taiwan_crude: Taiwan's crude imports suffered one of the steepest collapses in the dataset — plunging 66% from 864 kbd (9–15 Mar) to just 294 kbd (23–29 Mar), with Gulf share crashing from 66% to zero as Kuwait and Saudi Arabia both dropped out entirely.

The US is now Taiwan's sole crude supplier at ~294 kbd, a radical restructuring for an island that sourced ~70% of crude from the Middle East pre-crisis — but American barrels alone cannot feed CPC Corporation's 770 kbd refining capacity, leaving Taiwan running on strategic reserves.

With only ~150 days of oil reserves and Evergreen Marine's FM on all Gulf bookings, Taiwan faces an existential energy security test — the Atlantic Council warns the Iran war has exposed Taiwan's vulnerability to any chokepoint disruption, a lesson with direct implications for a potential China scenario.
taiwan_lng: Qatar's last LNG cargo to Taiwan arrived in 9–15 Mar (26.1 ktons/d, 38% share) — by 23–29 Mar, Qatar had completely disappeared following QatarEnergy's global FM extension, replaced by a diversified slate led by Australia (41.4 ktons/d, 58% share).

Taiwan successfully maintained total LNG imports at ~68–72 ktons/d through aggressive diversification: Brunei, Equatorial Guinea, Oman, US, and Canada all contributed cargoes, with Oman's non-Hormuz 10.4 Mtpa facility providing a critical Gulf-adjacent alternative.

The real risk is inventory depth — Taiwan holds only 11 days of natural gas reserves (vs Japan/Korea's weeks-to-months), meaning any supply disruption from Australia or a shipping delay could trigger power generation curtailments within days.
taiwan_gasoil_diesel: Taiwan's diesel imports fell 69% from 97 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 30 kbd (23–29 Mar), diversifying from South Korea alone to a mix of South Korea (18 kbd), China (12 kbd), and briefly Russia (4 kbd in 16–22 Mar).

The declining trend tracks Taiwan's crude supply collapse — as CPC Corporation reduces refinery throughput due to feedstock shortages, the island is simultaneously producing less diesel domestically and struggling to secure imported product.
taiwan_gasoline: Taiwan's gasoline imports are negligible and sporadic — a single 25 kbd Japanese cargo in 16–22 Mar, otherwise zero — reflecting CPC's refineries still covering domestic motor fuel needs from existing crude stocks, though this will become unsustainable as crude imports shrink.
taiwan_naphtha: Taiwan's naphtha imports collapsed from 147 kbd (9–15 Mar, 100% Gulf — UAE and Iraq) to zero by 23–29 Mar, a complete shutoff that directly threatens Formosa Plastics and other petrochemical giants that depend on Gulf naphtha feedstock.

Iraq's naphtha (20 kbd in W11) disappeared consistent with Iraq Oil Ministry's FM on all foreign-operated fields, while UAE naphtha (128 kbd → 95 kbd → 0) dried up as Hormuz transit became unviable — leaving Taiwan's petrochemical sector facing feedstock starvation with no alternative source in sight.
